summary: Andrew Carnegie argues that wealth inequality is a natural and beneficial byproduct of civilization's progress. He asserts that the wealthy have a moral obligation to live modestly and act as trustees of their surplus wealth, administering it during their lifetimes to fund public institutions that help the aspiring poor help themselves.

principles.statement: Great inequality of wealth is highly beneficial and essential for the progress of the human race.
principles.rationale: The accumulation of wealth by capable individuals drives down the cost of commodities, turning former luxuries into necessities. It also allows for the refinement of civilization, such as literature and the arts, which is preferable to universal squalor.
principles.source_quote: Much better this great irregularity than universal squalor. Without wealth there can be no Meccenas.

principles.statement: Surplus wealth should be administered by its possessor during their lifetime for the common good.
principles.rationale: When the wealthy actively administer their fortunes, the surplus of the few becomes the property of the many. The wealthy individual's superior wisdom and experience can direct these funds more effectively than if they were scattered in trifling amounts among the masses.
principles.source_quote: Under its sway we shall have an ideal State, in which the surplus wealth of the few will become, in the best sense, property of the many, because administering for the common good

principles.statement: Philanthropy should focus on providing the means for people to improve themselves, rather than giving direct handouts.
principles.rationale: Direct almsgiving does not improve the individual or the race. True charity involves building infrastructure (like libraries and parks) that acts as a ladder for those who desire to rise.
principles.source_quote: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves; to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so

frameworks.name: The Duty of the Man of Wealth
frameworks.when_to_apply: When a successful individual is deciding how to live and how to dispose of their accumulated fortune.
steps: Set an example of modest, unostentatious living, shunning display or extravagance.
Provide moderately for the legitimate wants of dependents.
Treat all remaining surplus revenues as trust funds.
Administer these trust funds during your lifetime in the manner best calculated to produce the most beneficial results for the community.
frameworks.source_quote: This, then, is held to be the duty of the man of wealth: To set an example of modest, unostentatious living, shunning display or extravagance; to provide moderately for the legitimate wants of those dependent upon him; and, after doing so, to consider all surplus revenues which come to him simply as trust funds...

mental_models.name: The Millionaire as Trustee
mental_models.description: Viewing the wealthy individual not as the absolute owner of their surplus, but as an agent or trustee holding funds temporarily. The trustee is obligated to distribute this wealth for the public good, leveraging their superior business acumen and experience to benefit the poor more effectively than the poor could do for themselves.
mental_models.example: Instead of paying higher wages or giving away small sums of money, a wealthy person acts as a trustee by pooling their surplus to build free libraries, parks, and works of art for the community.

heuristics: Help those who will help themselves.
Place within the community's reach the ladders upon which the aspiring can rise.
Consider surplus revenues simply as trust funds.

signature_quotes.text: The problem of our age is the proper administration of wealth, that the ties of brotherhood may still bind together the rich and poor in harmonious relationship.
signature_quotes.context: Opening sentence of the essay, establishing the core problem of modern capitalism.

signature_quotes.text: Much better this great irregularity than universal squalor.
signature_quotes.context: Defending the vast wealth inequality between the millionaire and the laborer as a necessary byproduct of civilization.

signature_quotes.text: In bestowing charity, the main consideration should be to help those who will help themselves; to provide part of the means by which those who desire to improve may do so; to give those who desire to rise the aids by which they may rise; to assist, but rarely or never to do all.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the proper method of philanthropy.

signature_quotes.text: ...the millionaire will be but a trustee for the poor, intrusted for a season with a great part of the increased wealth of the community, but administering it for the community far better than it could or would have done for itself.
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing the ideal state of wealth distribution.

anti_patterns.description: Almsgiving and direct charity (distributing wealth in trifling amounts to the masses).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Neither the individual nor the race is improved by almsgiving. It fails to encourage self-reliance, and the funds are less potent than when pooled by a wealthy administrator for large-scale public works.

anti_patterns.description: Leaving vast surplus wealth to families or bequeathing it for public purposes only after death.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It deprives the community of the wealthy individual's superior wisdom, experience, and ability to actively administer the funds during their lifetime for maximum societal benefit.
